subject: physics
educational_personality: exceptional classroom teacher; cream textbook; diagram teaches

lesson.big_idea: Light travels in straight lines. Shadows form when an opaque object blocks the path.

sections.title: Have You Wondered?
sections.body: Why is your shadow long in the evening and short at noon? Light and blockers tell the story.

sections.title: Using the Diagram
sections.body: Trace Source → Object → Shadow. The diagram shows light blocked and darkness on the other side.

sections.title: Concept: Light Path
sections.body: Light travels in straight lines called rays. Opaque objects stop those rays and cast shadows.

sections.title: Say It Simply
sections.body: Light goes straight. When something solid stands in the way, a shadow appears behind it.

sections.title: In Real Life
sections.body: A tree's shadow on a playground stretches as the sun lowers because the light arrives at a shallower angle.

sections.title: Worked Example
sections.body: Hold a pencil in front of a torch. Move the pencil closer to the wall and watch the shadow grow.

sections.title: Try This
sections.body: Point to Shadow on the diagram and explain why it appears opposite the light source.

sections.title: Lesson Summary
sections.body: Light travels straight. Opaque objects block light and create shadows we can predict.

sections.title: Think About It
sections.body: When is your shadow longest outdoors? Link that to the sun's position.

sections.title: Apply Your Learning
sections.body: Design a shadow-puppet scene that uses straight-line light to make clear shapes.

diagram_package.title: Light
diagram_package.caption: Source → Object → Shadow
diagram_package.explanation: This diagram teaches how the ideas in Light connect.
diagram_package.practice_question: Point to Source and explain how moving it changes the shadow.
